mainText:
Nepal's First Elections Since Last Year's Youth Uprising

On March 5, 2026, Nepal held its first parliamentary elections following the youth uprising that ousted Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli in September 2025. This uprising was sparked by a controversial social media ban intended to curb misinformation but perceived by many as an attempt to silence dissent. Protests erupted, leading to violence, a government resignation, and a transition to an interim government under Sushila Karki.

Voters went to the polls yesterday seeking a stable government amid hopes for reduced corruption. Early results indicate that former Kathmandu Mayor Balendra Shah, leading the Rastriya Swatantra Party, holds a commanding lead in the race, reportedly ahead in 105 of the 165 directly elected seats in Parliament.

Shah has emerged as a potential prime minister, having formerly partnered with youth during the protests against Oli's administration. His party currently surpasses Oli's Communist Party of Nepal (Unified Marxist-Leninist), which is trailing with only 11 seats. The National Democratic Congress and the populist Shram Sanskriti Party follow in third and fourth places, respectively.

Election turnout was approximately 58%, a slight decline from the 61.6% seen in 2022. Despite the drop, analysts suggest that the results reflect a rejection of Oli's previous governance, which was marked by significant unrest following the social media law that rendered major platforms like Facebook and Instagram inaccessible.

In addition to Shah's likely lead, he is ahead in his constituency of Jhapa-5, with more than 6,500 votes compared to Oli's 1,400. With early vote counts running smoothly, final results are expected to be announced within the week.

Amid this political shift, Nepalese citizens express a demand for a government that prioritizes anti-corruption measures and governmental accountability. Many view the uprising not just as a reaction to censorship but as a broader rejection of the corruption and elitism that has plagued their government for years.

International Affairs Update

In other news, U.S. administration policy changes have made headlines this week. Following Iranian drone strikes, Qatar suspended its liquefied natural gas production. The Pentagon has designated AI company Anthropic as a supply chain risk, and shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has nearly halted amid continued Iranian attacks.

Also, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has stated that several nations are seeking Ukraine's help to defend against Iranian Shahed drones, which have been utilized in attacks against Ukrainian targets. These developments highlight the ongoing geopolitical tensions involving Iran, the region, and Ukraine on the global stage, as nations navigate the effects of these conflicts on international stability.
